slides.title: We’ll begin with the fundamentals — systems, Fourier analysis, signal processing
slides.fullText: We’ll begin with the fundamentals — systems, Fourier analysis, signal processing — and hands-on MATLAB sessions. Then we’ll explore major imaging techniques: X-ray and radiography, CT, PET, SPECT, MRI, ultrasound, optical, and deep imaging.
slides.script: We'll start with the building blocks of medical imaging, which are systems, Fourier analysis, and signal processing. These fundamentals will give us a solid foundation for understanding how imaging systems work and how they produce images. We'll also have hands-on experience with MATLAB to reinforce our understanding of these concepts.

slides.title: So, to kick things off, I want to frame today’s introduction around four simple
bullets: First: What is this course about?

At its core, medical imaging is about inner vision. It’s the set of ideas and tools that lets us “see” inside the body, not by opening it up, but by using physics, computation, and instrumentation to form images from measured signals.
Second: Why study medical imaging?

There are two big reasons. One is health-related knowledge. Medical imaging sits right at the center of modern diagnosis, treatment planning, and disease monitoring, so understanding it gives you a practical way to connect engineering and science to real clinical impact.

The other is career requirements. If you plan to work in biomedical engineering, radiology-related technology, AI for healthcare, medical devices, or research labs, imaging literacy is not optional. It’s a foundational skill set.
Third: Who is this course for?

There are two sides here. We, the instructors, will guide you through the fundamentals and help you build the right intuition. But the other “who” is you. Your effort matters a lot in this course, because imaging becomes clear only when you actively work through the concepts and practice applying them.
Finally: How are we going to learn this?
We’ll build things up from the foundation, and then move through the major modalities one by one, so you understand both the common principles and what makes each imaging method unique.

You’ll also do hands-on work in MATLAB, because imaging is something you learn by doing, not just reading. And along the way, we’ll use books and online resources, including targeted searches, to help you develop the habit of learning and verifying information the way real researchers and engineers do.
That’s the roadmap: inner vision, why it matters, who’s involved, and how we’ll approach it step by step.

slides.title: Let’s build on this idea of medical imaging as inner vision, but now I want to p
bullets: As humans, our natural vision is impressive, but it has a hard limit. We can see surfaces and appearances, but we cannot directly see what’s hidden inside most objects, and certainly not inside the body. If something is behind a wall, inside a closed box, or deep within tissue, visible light does not help us much.

Medical imaging is our way of breaking that limitation. It gives us a principled, scientific way to “see the unseen”, to recover internal structure and function from measurements, without cutting the body open. That’s why this slide calls it mystery vision: we are turning hidden internal reality into something observable and measurable.
And notice the line on the slide: IT = Industrial, Info, Imaging, Intelligent Tech.
This is a reminder that what we are doing in this course is not only medicine. Medical imaging sits at the intersection of:
Industrial tech: the hardware, sensors, scanners, and real systems that must work reliably.
Information tech: signals, data, computation, and how we process measurements into images.
Imaging tech: the physics and mathematics of image formation and reconstruction.
Intelligent tech: algorithms and AI that help interpret images, improve quality, and support decisions.
So when a patient has symptoms and a physician needs answers, imaging becomes a kind of extended vision, a controlled way to look beneath the surface, quantify what’s happening, and guide care.
That is the big message here: medical imaging is mystery vision made scientific, and it lives at the crossroads of industrial systems, information, imaging physics, and intelligent computation.

spotlightEvents.label: Principled Way to See the Unseen

slides.title: Let me push this idea one step further. If “mystery vision” is about seeing what
bullets: Think about the superhero idea on this slide: X-ray vision. The fantasy is that you can look through the body and immediately understand what’s going on inside. In real life, we obviously do not have magical eyes, but medical imaging gives us the next best thing: technology-assisted vision that can reveal internal anatomy and, in many cases, function, without surgery.

And what’s important is that this “super vision” is not one single tool. It comes in different forms, depending on what you want to see. Some modalities are best for structure, like bones or organs. Others are better for soft tissue detail, blood flow, metabolism, or motion. Each modality gives you a different kind of window into the body.

So the goal of this course is to understand how we achieve that super vision in a scientific way: what we measure, how we form an image from those measurements, what the limitations are, and how clinicians use the results to make decisions.

What once sounded like science fiction is now routine clinical practice. Imaging helps detect disease earlier, guide treatment more precisely, and monitor changes over time. And as we go through the course, you’ll see how those capabilities became possible, and why they matter in real healthcare.

slides.title: Now, let’s go back to where it all began. In 1895, a German physicist named Wilh
bullets: To test his discovery, he took the first X-ray image—a picture of his wife’s hand. You could clearly see the bones, and even her wedding ring. It was the first time humans saw inside the body without surgery. That moment changed medicine forever.
X-rays could depict dense structures like bones and metal with incredible clarity. This breakthrough was so significant that Roentgen received a Nobel Prize in 1901.
That single discovery launched the field of radiology—and over a century later, we’re still building on it.
slides.fullText:
Now, let’s go back to where it all began. In 1895, a German physicist named Wilhelm Conrad Roentgen was experimenting with cathode rays when he discovered something totally unexpected—an invisible form of radiation that could pass through solid objects. He called them X-rays because he didn’t quite know what they were.

To test his discovery, he took the first X-ray image—a picture of his wife’s hand. You could clearly see the bones, and even her wedding ring. It was the first time humans saw inside the body without surgery. That moment changed medicine forever.

X-rays could depict dense structures like bones and metal with incredible clarity. This breakthrough was so significant that Roentgen received a Nobel Prize in 1901.

That single discovery launched the field of radiology—and over a century later, we’re still building on it.
slides.script: Wilhelm Conrad Roentgen's discovery of X-rays in 1895 revolutionized medicine by allowing us to see inside the body without surgery. He accidentally discovered X-rays while experimenting with cathode rays and named them X-rays because he didn't know what they were. The first X-ray image was taken of his wife's hand, revealing the bones and even her wedding ring, and this moment changed the course of medical history.

slides.title: Fast forward to today, and X-ray imaging—specifically X-ray radiography—is one o
bullets: Let’s say someone falls and hurts their arm. An X-ray can quickly show whether there's a fracture. Or in breast cancer screening, mammography—a specialized type of X-ray radiography—can reveal tiny calcifications or early tumors that might not be detectable by touch. Early detection like this can save lives.

But there’s a limitation with X-ray radiographs. They’re 2D projections, so everything in the body along the X-ray path gets stacked into one image. That makes it hard to see soft tissues clearly or to isolate specific structures.

So, the next logical step was to ask—what if we could look from multiple directions and reconstruct a full 3D view of what’s inside? That question led to the development of Computed Tomography, or CT, which we’ll get into next.
slides.fullText:
Fast forward to today, and X-ray imaging—specifically X-ray radiography—is one of the most common tools used in hospitals and clinics around the world. It’s fast, affordable, and incredibly useful.

Let’s say someone falls and hurts their arm. An X-ray can quickly show whether there's a fracture. Or in breast cancer screening, mammography—a specialized type of X-ray radiography—can reveal tiny calcifications or early tumors that might not be detectable by touch. Early detection like this can save lives.

But there’s a limitation with X-ray radiographs. They’re 2D projections, so everything in the body along the X-ray path gets stacked into one image. That makes it hard to see soft tissues clearly or to isolate specific structures.

So, the next logical step was to ask—what if we could look from multiple directions and reconstruct a full 3D view of what’s inside? That question led to the development of Computed Tomography, or CT, which we’ll get into next.
slides.script: X-ray radiography is a fundamental tool in hospitals and clinics due to its speed, affordability, and effectiveness. It's used to quickly diagnose conditions like fractures or early signs of breast cancer, such as tiny calcifications or tumors. However, X-ray radiographs have a limitation, as they are 2D projections, showing superimposed structures that can make it difficult to interpret the image.

slides.title: Alright, so earlier we talked about how regular X-rays give us a flat, two-dimen
bullets: Now imagine if we could take many X-ray radiographs around the body—virtually, of course—and figure out what’s happening on transverse sections. That’s exactly what computed tomography, or CT, allows us to do.

The word "tomography" comes from Greek—"tomos" means slice, and "graphy" means writing or drawing. So, we're basically drawing slices of the body. With CT, we collect X-ray projections from many angles, then use a computer to reconstruct a cross-sectional image.

The result? A crystal-clear view of internal anatomy, without overlapping tissues. Today’s CT scanners are incredibly precise—some can even resolve features smaller than a millimeter. Think of it like slicing a watermelon and looking inside but doing it without a knife—just scanning. It’s an amazing tool, and one that has completely transformed modern medicine.

slides.title: So far, we've focused on X-rays—but medical imaging goes way beyond that. In fac
bullets: Depending on what part of the spectrum you use, you get different kinds of information. For example:
Gamma rays are used in nuclear imaging, like PET and SPECT
Radio waves are instrumental in MRI
Visible and infrared light are for optical imaging
And even ultrasound, though not part of the EM spectrum, gives us mechanical wave-based imaging, referred to as ultrasound imaging.

Each imaging modality has its own strengths and weaknesses. Some are great for structure, others for function. Some are good at seeing bones, others at detecting molecular processes like metabolism or gene expression.

In fact, no single technique gives us the whole picture. That’s why modern medicine often combines them—what we call multimodal imaging. When we link them together, we get a rather rich view of what’s going on inside the body.

Different parts of the spectrum provide different kinds of information, such as gamma rays for nuclear imaging, radio waves for MRI, visible and infrared light for optical imaging, and ultrasound for mechanical wave-based imaging. Each modality has its own strengths and weaknesses, making them suitable for specific uses.

spotlightEvents.label: Visible and infrared light in optical imaging

slides.title: Let’s talk a bit more about what makes tomography special.
bullets: When you take a regular photo with your phone, you get a picture—that’s it. It’s a direct result of what the camera sees. But tomographic imaging is different. You don’t directly capture the image you want. Instead, you collect data from many different angles, and that data is a kind of indirect measurement—a projection of what's inside.

Now here’s the key part: to get a cross-sectional image, we need to do something called inversion. In other words, we take the measurements and reverse-engineer the internal structure that produced them.

This is an inverse problem. It’s different from the more familiar forward problem, where you know all the inputs, and a forward model, and calculate the outcome. Like in physics: you know the force and the mass, so you calculate how something moves.
In tomography, it’s the opposite. You see the outcome—the measurement—and you ask: what could have caused this? That’s a lot harder.

Here’s a fun analogy: think of boiling an egg. That’s a straightforward process. But what if someone gave you a boiled egg and said, “Unboil it”? That’s the inverse problem. Sounds impossible—but researchers have found ways to reverse that process under specific conditions. It’s challenging, but not hopeless. And that’s what makes inversion in imaging both difficult and exciting.

slides.title: Here’s another example that might feel a bit more familiar.
bullets: Let’s say you take a photo with your phone, but it turns out a little blurry—maybe the camera shook, or the focus was off, or the camera is cheap and of poor quality. What you get is not the true image, but a version that’s been blurred in several ways.

Now, how do we describe that blurring? In image processing, we model it using something called a point spread function—or PSF for short. This describes how a single point of light spreads out in the image.

The process that causes blurring is called convolution. It’s a mathematical operation that combines the original image with the PSF. Convolution is sort of like multiplication—but in the so-called Fourier space that we will explain later in this course. Indeed, in the Fourier domain, convolution becomes regular multiplication. The Fourier transform is often performed using a fast algorithm, FFT in short. That simplifies a lot of computations.
But now comes the hard part: what if all you have is the blurry image? Can you work backward and recover the original, sharp version?

That’s another inverse problem. And just like with tomography, it’s tricky. You're trying to undo the effect of the blur, based on your knowledge of the system. It takes clever algorithms and good models—but it can be done. And that’s the kind of thinking we’ll develop throughout this course.

slides.script: Let's break down the blurring process step by step. When a point of light passes through the camera's lens, it gets smeared out, forming a blurry spot. This spot is the point spread function, or PSF. The PSF is then applied to every pixel in the image through a process called convolution, which combines the PSF with the original image to produce the final, blurry result. Convolution is a fundamental concept in image processing, and it's used to model all sorts of image degradation, from motion blur to noise.

slides.title: Let’s take a step back for a moment and think about how mathematics builds over
bullets: Start with addition. At this level, we’re not just adding a few numbers. We might be adding up contributions continuously over space or time. That’s what integration is: a continuous version of addition, like the “area under the curve” idea shown here.

Next, instead of simple subtraction, we start focusing on change. In calculus, that leads to derivatives, where we look at how a quantity changes locally. Geometrically, that’s why you see the picture of a curve with a tangent line: the derivative is the slope of that tangent, capturing local behavior at a point.

Now take multiplication, but in imaging and signal processing. A key idea is that an imaging system often “spreads” or “mixes” information from nearby points, and we model that using convolution. That’s why one of the pictures shows a blurred image: blur is a typical result of convolution with a system response. And under standard assumptions for linear, shift-invariant systems, convolution in the spatial domain corresponds to multiplication in the Fourier domain.

Finally, we get to division, and this is the main point of the slide: division is harder. In imaging, “division” often means trying to reverse a forward process, like deblurring. You are given a blurred measurement and asked to recover the original, and that becomes an inverse problem. It is typically sensitive to noise and may not have a unique solution unless we add constraints or regularization.
And that’s exactly the mindset behind many medical imaging methods, especially tomography: we measure indirect data, then solve an inverse problem to reconstruct what’s actually inside.

Next, instead of simple subtraction, we start focusing on change. In calculus, that leads to derivatives, where we look at how a quantity changes locally. Geometrically, that’s why you see the picture of a curve with a tangent line: the derivative is the slope of that tangent, capturing local behavior at a point.

Now take multiplication, but in imaging and signal processing. A key idea is that an imaging system often “spreads” or “mixes” information from nearby points, and we model that using convolution. That’s why one of the pictures shows a blurred image: blur is a typical result of convolution with a system response. slides.title: Now, here’s the key message of this slide: inversion is hard, much harder than t
bullets: Look at the pictures. On the left, you see the Eiffel Tower the normal way, upright and familiar. That’s like a forward problem: you start with a known object and a known physical process, and you can predict what you will observe. Forward modeling is usually stable and straightforward because you control the rules.

On the right, the tower is flipped and looks unnatural. That’s a simple visual reminder of what an inverse problem feels like. In an inverse problem, you start from what you observed, often incomplete and noisy, and you try to work backward to recover the underlying cause. And that backward step is where difficulties show up.
Why is it tricky? Because:
there may be many different causes that could explain similar observations,
real measurements include noise and missing information,
and small errors in the data can lead to big errors in the reconstructed result unless you add constraints.

So in practice, solving an inverse problem means you need more than one tool. You combine mathematics, algorithms, and physics-based models, and you often need some form of prior knowledge or regularization to guide the solution toward something realistic.

And that’s why this course is not just about definitions. It’s about learning how to think quantitatively about these forward and inverse relationships, because medical imaging is full of inversion problems. It’s challenging, but once you get the intuition, it’s also one of the most powerful ideas in modern engineering and medicine.

slides.title: Now let’s take a moment to appreciate just how interdisciplinary medical imaging
bullets: We’re combining mathematics, physics, chemistry, biology, and engineering—all in one field. Think about that. Whether we’re modeling how X-rays interact with tissue, analyzing ultrasound signals, or designing contrast agents for optical imaging—we’re pulling from all corners of science and technology.

This means that medical imaging isn’t just about one skill set. It’s about thinking broadly and connecting the dots between different fields. And if you enjoy solving complex problems, this is one of the best places to be.

So by now, you should have a pretty good sense of what this course is about—and what makes it so special. We’ll focus on modern biomedical imaging technologies, and we’ll build the knowledge and tools needed to understand how they work, and how they’re used to help people.
And honestly, it’s really cool stuff.

slides.title: So by now, you might be thinking—this is all very cool, but why do I really need
bullets: Well, I’d say there are at least two solid reasons.

First—and perhaps most importantly—it gives you essential health-related knowledge. Whether you pursue a career in engineering, medicine, or something entirely different, understanding how imaging works can help you make sense of your own health and the health of those around you, during your hospital visits and afterwards.

The second reason is career-related. We’ll get to that more. But for now, just keep in mind—this is cool knowledge for a niche group of people. It’s personal and extremely useful in several ways; to say the least, it is a required course for your college education.

slides.title: Let me share something personal with you.
bullets: A couple of years ago, I had a kidney stone. Not fun. I ended up in the hospital, they injected a contrast agent, ran a CT scan, and just like that—they could see the size and location of the stone.
Now, because I understood what was going on, I wasn’t worried or confused. I could follow the diagnosis, and I knew what the doctors were looking for.

And honestly, sooner or later, most of us will have a medical scan—whether it’s an X-ray, a CT, or an MRI. And perhaps, all of them and multiple times. It could be you, or someone you love. When that happens, wouldn’t it be helpful to understand what those images mean?
That’s why this knowledge matters—not just in theory, but in real life.

slides.title: Let’s shift to a more personal motivation, which is what this slide is pointing
bullets: At some point, many of you will have a family, or you already do. And sooner or later, someone close to you may need an X-ray, a CT, an MRI, an ultrasound, or some kind of imaging test. When that happens, it helps a lot if you can understand the basic idea of what the scan is for, what information it can and cannot provide, and what the report language generally means.

Even if you never become a radiologist or an imaging researcher, this course can still empower you. You will be able to communicate more effectively with clinicians, ask better questions, and avoid being completely lost when imaging results come up in a real medical situation.
So that’s one reason to study medical imaging: personal and practical value.
Now let’s move to the second reason, which is your career.

slides.title: Let’s zoom out for a moment and look at biomedical engineering as a field.
bullets: It’s one of the newest branches of engineering. Engineering started with disciplines like civil and mechanical, then moved into electrical, chemical, and aerospace. More recently, biomedical engineering emerged to bridge the gap between technology and medicine.
And it’s grown fast—because it’s relevant, it’s interdisciplinary, and it’s impactful.

In many universities, biomedical engineering includes at least two major focus areas. One is imaging, which is what we’re covering here. The other is tissue engineering and regenerative medicine, often called TERM.

Whether or not you plan to specialize in imaging, this course plays a foundational role. It builds your technical knowledge, strengthens your systems thinking, and helps you understand one of the most important technologies in healthcare today.
So if you’re here as part of your degree requirements—great. But don’t just treat it like a checkbox. Learn it well. It will pay off, academically, professionally, and personally.

slides.title: Now let’s talk about the second motivation: your career, which is exactly what t
bullets: Look at this image. This is not a research lab. It’s an industry setting, a company showcase, with a clinical imaging system on display, in this case a CT scanner. The point is simple: medical imaging is a real, large-scale industry, and it needs people who understand both the engineering and the medical context.

If you take this course seriously, it opens multiple career paths. You could work on the hardware side, like detectors, system design, and instrumentation. You could work on the software side, like reconstruction algorithms, signal processing, and image analysis. And increasingly, you could work on AI for imaging: improving image quality, helping with triage, or supporting clinical decision-making. Hospitals, medical device companies, and research groups all need these skills.

So even though medical imaging is technical, that’s exactly why it is valuable. If you can explain how an imaging system produces an image, what its limitations are, and how to validate performance, you become useful in industry, healthcare technology, and research.

And yes, biomedical engineering and imaging-related work is a strong professional direction because it naturally sits at the intersection of medicine, life science, and engineering, and those intersections are where many high-impact jobs are created.

bullets: Now, the exact numbers in the job market change over time, but the bigger message is still important: healthcare has been steadily becoming more technology-driven. Devices, imaging systems, sensors, and data-driven medicine all create demand for people who can connect biology, medicine, and engineering.

At the same time, it’s also worth being honest about the present-day outlook. The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics currently projects employment for bioengineers and biomedical engineers to grow 5% from 2024 to 2034, which is still faster than average, but not the explosive growth shown in that older 2008–2018 projection.

So the takeaway is: biomedical engineering can be a strong path because it sits where medicine and technology meet, and it tends to offer solid compensation as well. For context, BLS wage data for this occupation reports a median around $99,550 (May 2022)

bullets: This figure is a country-level market view broken into services and products, and it compares an earlier baseline year (2019) with a projected later year (2027). If you are interested in industry, academia, or even building new tools, medical imaging is a field with real scale and continued demand.

slides.title: Now let’s connect the medical imaging market to the major modalities you’ll lear
bullets: This chart breaks the global market down by Ultrasound, CT, MRI, X-ray, and Other types, and it shows a steady increase over time. The total market size grows from about $29.2B in 2022 to a projected $48.8B by 2032, with a stated CAGR of 5.4%.

What I want you to notice is not just the total growth, but the fact that every major modality contributes to the overall expansion. Ultrasound, CT, MRI, and X-ray are all foundational technologies in clinical care, and the market growth reflects how broadly they are used across different departments and patient needs.

And “Other types” is a useful reminder that imaging is not limited to the big four. As the field evolves, new techniques and specialized systems continue to emerge, and they gradually become part of the clinical and commercial ecosystem.

So the takeaway here is simple: medical imaging is not a niche area. Across modalities, there is sustained investment and long-term demand. That’s one more reason the fundamentals you learn in this course, physics, systems, reconstruction, and interpretation, stay highly relevant.

slides.title: So far, we’ve talked about medical imaging in terms of impact and even market gr
bullets: This slide summarizes a physician survey from 2001, where physicians ranked major medical innovations. And what’s striking is the number one choice: MRI and CT scanning.

In this exhibit, MRI and CT scanning are ranked #1, with a mean score of 0.878, and 75.6% of respondents selected it as the “most” important innovation. That is an unusually strong result, and it tells you something about how central these technologies are to modern medicine.

Why would CT and MRI stand out so clearly? Because they fundamentally changed diagnosis. They give us noninvasive, internal, cross-sectional views of the body, often with enough detail to localize disease, guide treatment decisions, and monitor progression over time. In many clinical scenarios, having CT or MRI is the difference between guessing and knowing.

And that’s the motivation for this course: we’re not just learning what these systems are. We’re going to learn how they produce images, what assumptions they rely on, what limitations they have, and why reconstruction, the inverse problem, is so important. Once you understand that, you can explain both the engineering and the clinical value with real confidence.

slides.title: Let me show you how the course is organized, using this schedule as our roadmap.
bullets: You can see two big phases.

First is the foundation phase, shown here in the dark blue block. This is where we build the mathematical and conceptual toolkit that almost every imaging method depends on. We start with System, then Fourier Series, Signal Processing, Convolution, Fourier Transform, and Discrete Fourier Transform and FFT. We also include topics like Network, because modern imaging systems are not just physics and math, they are also data pipelines and interconnected components.

We structure it this way because students come in with different backgrounds. Some of you have already seen linear systems and Fourier analysis. Others may be seeing these ideas for the first time. That’s completely normal in an interdisciplinary field like medical imaging. The goal of the foundation phase is to get everyone to the point where these core ideas feel clear and usable, not mysterious.

You’ll notice MATLAB sessions highlighted in yellow. Early on we have MatLab I (Basics), and later we have MatLab II (Homework) and MatLab III (CT). These are placed intentionally, because imaging is something you learn by doing. MATLAB gives us a practical way to simulate systems, visualize signals, and later connect the math directly to reconstruction problems.
You’ll also notice a No Class day on 2/20, and then Exam I on 2/23, which marks a natural checkpoint right after the foundation block.

After that, we move into the modality phase, shown in green. This is where we apply the foundation ideas to real imaging technologies: X-ray and Radiography, CT Reconstruction, CT Scanner, then Nuclear Physics leading into PET and SPECT. After that we cover MRI I, MRI II, MRI III, followed by Ultrasound I and II, and finally Optical Imaging I and II.
And just like you see on the schedule, there are additional checkpoints: Exam II on 4/03, and Exam III on 4/27.

So the main message of this slide is: we’re not jumping straight into scanners on day one. We start by building the “language” of imaging, systems, convolution, and Fourier ideas, and then we use that language to understand each modality in a much deeper and more connected way.

As we go, keep an eye on the early foundation topics, especially Fourier concepts. They show up repeatedly later, particularly in CT and MRI, and the students who do best are usually the ones who invest early in making those fundamentals solid.

slides.title: In addition to our course materials, I’d like to mention a particularly helpful
bullets: Stanford University offers an excellent course titled 'EE261: The Fourier Transform and Its Applications'. It’s taught by Professor Brad Osgood, and the best part is that it is freely available online through Stanford’s SEE platform. You can access the full textbook there and watch high-quality lecture videos.

Now, a quick word of caution: the Stanford course spans an entire semester. The book is over 400 pages long. Don’t feel overwhelmed. You absolutely do not need to cover all of it. Think of it as a reference companion to our course. In fact, for our purposes, reading even 80–100 pages from selected sections will be more than enough to reinforce your understanding.

What makes this resource valuable is the clarity and consistency of its explanations and mathematical treatment. It builds the theory from the ground up, with elegant examples and a style that’s both rigorous and readable. If you ever find yourself puzzled by a concept I introduce—say, the derivation of the Fourier series, or the intuition behind convolution—this book can offer a second viewpoint. Sometimes, a slightly different presentation is all you need for clarity.

However, please note that we will follow our own structured path in this course. For example, I introduce linear systems before Fourier transforms, because in imaging, systems thinking provides the conceptual grounding. The Stanford course takes the reverse order. Both are valid—but here, we start with linear systems to better prepare for their applications in imaging modalities like CT and MRI.

Please use this Stanford material as a valuable supplement, not a replacement. You’ll benefit most by following our course flow carefully and referring to the Stanford book only when you want to go deeper or review challenging topics.

Lastly, although I have reached out for formal permission, the material seems publicly accessible and openly shared for educational use. So, feel free to explore it from your own dorm room, your library, or anywhere you study.
This is an exciting time to learn these topics—tools like ChatGPT make it easier than ever to gain a deeper understanding. Let’s use them wisely.

slides.title: Continuing from our previous discussions, let’s dive into the physics of X-ray i
bullets: In X-ray imaging, we start with the concept of a line integral. Essentially, the X-rays pass through the object, and what we measure is the energy of penetrated x-ray photons and then compute the line integral along the x-ray path. Such line integrals together form the Radon transform. To make it simpler, imagine you have an object, and you put all line integrals from a specific direction to form an x-ray projection. This projection is a one-dimensional profile, which is the information you can get along all the X-ray paths passing through the object in that direction.
Now, let’s take this idea into the realm of mathematics. We start with a 2D image, denoted as

f(x,y). When we have all x-ray projections or the Radon transform, we get a new 2D function  p(θ,t), where  t is the coordinate along the 1D detector array and  θ is the projection orientation. By changing the angle θ, we rotate the data acquisition system to gather x-ray projections.

Now, the magic happens when we flip the process around. Given this collected projection dataset, our goal is to reconstruct the original 2D image f(x,y). This is the inverse process, and here’s where things get interesting: by perform the 1D Fourier trans of each and every of the 1D projections, we move into the Fourier domain.

To put it simply, the 1D projections we collect represent profiles in the frequency domain. By rotating the data acquisition system, we eventually capture the entire Fourier space. And once we have all the data in the Fourier space, we can apply the inverse Fourier transform to reconstruct the original image. This process connects the data from the physical object to the Fourier spectrum of the underlying image, and from the Fourier spectrum, we can reconstruct the original image.

While this overview gives you the fundamental idea, later in the course, we’ll dive deeper into the Fourier transform, why they can be inverted, and how this mathematical framework ties directly to the imaging process. But for now, keep in mind that the forward process (how we collect data) and the inverse process (how we reconstruct the image) are two sides of the same coin, closely linked by Fourier analysis.

We start with the concept of a line integral, where X-rays pass through the object, and we measure the energy of penetrated X-ray photons to compute the line integral along the X-ray path. These line integrals together form the Radon transform, which is a mathematical representation of the X-ray projections.

slides.title: Continuing with our exploration of imaging modalities, let’s now discuss Positro
bullets: So, how does it work? Well, the key to PET is the introduction of radioactive tracers into the body. These tracers are typically radioactive chemicals that participate in your body’s metabolic processes. For example, tumors tend to consume a lot of glucose. If we couple glucose molecules with a radioactive tracer, we can track how much glucose the tumor is using, which directly correlates with its activity. Tumors, which are often highly metabolic, will naturally absorb more of this tracer.

Once the PET tracer is in the body, it begins to emit gamma ray photons in pairs, and two photons in a pair will travel in opposite directions. Gamma rays, much like X-rays, can penetrate through tissues and be detected externally. When we receive gamma ray photons from two different detector locations, we know they originated from the same event somewhere on the line connecting the two detectors.

This process is similar to the measurement of line integrals used for CT. Then, we can apply Fourier analysis to reconstruct the images. In PET, Fourier transforms help us create detailed, functional images of the body’s processes. The goal here is to understand how much activities are happening at specific locations. So, while we’ve seen how CT gives us anatomical details, PET allows us to observe the biological functions—like metabolism—within those structures.

This modality is especially useful for identifying cancerous tissues, studying brain function, and even monitoring heart conditions. Later, we’ll explain how the gamma ray photons are detected and processed to reconstruct these functional images. But for now, remember that PET imaging provides us with a way to look inside the body not just for its structure, but also for how physiology is actively working.
slides.fullText:
Continuing with our exploration of imaging modalities, let’s now discuss Positron Emission Tomography, or PET. While the previous imaging techniques focused on capturing the anatomical structure, PET offers a powerful way to visualize the functional activity in the human body.

So, how does it work? Well, the key to PET is the introduction of radioactive tracers into the body. These tracers are typically radioactive chemicals that participate in your body’s metabolic processes. For example, tumors tend to consume a lot of glucose. If we couple glucose molecules with a radioactive tracer, we can track how much glucose the tumor is using, which directly correlates with its activity. Tumors, which are often highly metabolic, will naturally absorb more of this tracer.

Once the PET tracer is in the body, it begins to emit gamma ray photons in pairs, and two photons in a pair will travel in opposite directions. Gamma rays, much like X-rays, can penetrate through tissues and be detected externally. When we receive gamma ray photons from two different detector locations, we know they originated from the same event somewhere on the line connecting the two detectors.
This process is similar to the measurement of line integrals used for CT. Then, we can apply Fourier analysis to reconstruct the images. In PET, Fourier transforms help us create detailed, functional images of the body’s processes. The goal here is to understand how much activities are happening at specific locations. So, while we’ve seen how CT gives us anatomical details, PET allows us to observe the biological functions—like metabolism—within those structures.

This modality is especially useful for identifying cancerous tissues, studying brain function, and even monitoring heart conditions. Later, we’ll explain how the gamma ray photons are detected and processed to reconstruct these functional images. But for now, remember that PET imaging provides us with a way to look inside the body not just for its structure, but also for how physiology is actively working.
slides.script: PET is a functional imaging modality that visualizes the body's activity by introducing radioactive tracers, which participate in metabolic processes, allowing us to track the body's functional activity. These tracers accumulate in areas of high metabolic activity, such as tumors, enabling us to observe the body's functional processes. The most common tracer used in PET is fluorodeoxyglucose, or FDG, which accumulates in tumors due to their high glucose consumption.

slides.title: Now let’s look at a closely related nuclear medicine modality: Single Photon Emi
bullets: The key idea is in the name: single-photon emission. In SPECT, a radiotracer inside the body emits gamma photons one at a time, in random directions. That is different from PET, where the physics produces two photons that are emitted nearly back-to-back, and we can use coincidence detection to define a line of response more directly.

In SPECT, because the photons are not paired, we need another way to estimate where they came from. That’s why SPECT systems use a collimator, which is basically a physical filter in front of the detector that only allows photons traveling in certain directions to pass through. So when the detector records a photon, we don’t know its exact origin, but we do know it most likely traveled along a specific direction set by the collimator geometry.
If you look at the slide, you can see both sides of the story:
on the left, an example of the functional image you get from tracer uptake, and
on the right, the SPECT scanner, essentially a gamma camera system that rotates around the patient to collect many projection views.

Just like CT, the workflow is: collect projections from multiple angles, then use tomographic reconstruction to estimate the 3D distribution of tracer activity. The reconstruction details differ from PET because the measurement physics differs, but the overall inverse-problem flavor is the same.

A practical takeaway is this: SPECT is widely used clinically and can be more accessible than PET in many settings, but because it relies on collimation, it typically sacrifices a lot of photons, which often means lower sensitivity and resolution compared to PET. Still, it provides extremely valuable functional information, and that’s why it remains a major workhorse in nuclear medicine.

slides.script: SPECT is a closely related nuclear medicine modality that uses single-photon emission, where a radiotracer inside the body emits gamma photons one at a time, in random directions. Unlike PET, where paired photons are emitted, SPECT relies on a collimator to guide the photons toward the detector, estimating their origin. This is because the single-photon emission in SPECT does not provide the same level of directional information as the paired photons in PET.

slides.title: Now, let’s turn our attention to the third major imaging modality: Magnetic Reso
bullets: Here’s how it works: First, we subject the patient to a very strong magnetic field. This magnetic field aligns the hydrogen nuclei—which are abundant in water molecules in your body. Then, we introduce a radio frequency (RF) signal that briefly disrupts this alignment, causing the hydrogen nuclei to emit their own radio frequency signals as they return to their original stable state.

The key advantage of MRI is that it is sensitive to the water content in different tissues and interactions of hydrogen nuclei with their micro-environments, which makes MRI extremely useful for imaging soft tissues like the brain, muscles, and organs. The signal we collect can give us a lot of valuable information, such as chemical shifts in tissues, which help us distinguish between different types of tissues and even detect abnormalities like tumors or inflammation.

MRI is also great for studying brain activity, as the technique can be used to observe changes in blood flow over time. This makes MRI not only a structural imaging tool but also a functional one, adding a layer of depth to our understanding of the body.

Together with CT and nuclear imaging techniques, MRI forms the trio of most important imaging modalities. While CT focuses on capturing detailed anatomical structures using X-rays, and nuclear imaging provides insight into physiological functions, MRI offers unmatched soft tissue contrast and the ability to examine both structure and function in a non-invasive way, even into our thinking processes.

slides.title: Now, let's look at Ultrasound Imaging. While it might not be as widely discussed
bullets: So, how does ultrasound work? It operates based on the principle of mechanical vibrations. In ultrasound imaging, we use a device called a transducer, which contains piezoelectric materials. These materials can generate high-frequency sound waves when subjected to an electric current. These sound waves are then sent into the body.

As the sound waves travel through tissues, they interact with various structures inside the body. Some waves are reflected, and others continue through the tissues. The reflected waves are detected by the transducer, and from this data, we can reconstruct an image of the internal structures. Essentially, we’re mapping the body’s anatomy based on how sound waves bounce off tissues.

One of the greatest benefits of ultrasound is that it’s non-invasive and completely safe—unlike X-rays or CT scans, ultrasound doesn't involve ionizing radiation. It’s also incredibly cost-effective and high-speed, which makes it a go-to tool for many real-time imaging tasks. You’ll often see it used during fetal imaging in pregnancy, as well as in blood flow monitoring and guiding biopsies.

While it may not provide the same level of detail as MRI or CT, ultrasound’s affordability, speed, and safety make it a valuable tool for many clinical applications. Coupled with AI methods, ultrasound imaging has yet more to offer.

Now, let's look at Ultrasound Imaging. These materials can generate high-frequency sound waves when subjected to an electric current. These sound waves are then sent into the body.

The reflected waves are detected by the transducer, and from this data, we can reconstruct an image of the internal structures. Essentially, we’re mapping the body’s anatomy based on how sound waves bounce off tissues.

One of the greatest benefits of ultrasound is that it’s non-invasive and completely safe—unlike X-rays or CT scans, ultrasound doesn't involve ionizing radiation. It’s also incredibly cost-effective and high-speed, which makes it a go-to tool for many real-time imaging tasks. You’ll often see it used during fetal imaging in pregnancy, as well as in blood flow monitoring and guiding biopsies.

slides.script: Ultrasound imaging relies on mechanical vibrations to operate, using a transducer with piezoelectric materials that convert electrical signals into high-frequency sound waves. When these sound waves hit tissue boundaries, they produce echoes, which are then detected by the same transducer, converting the mechanical vibrations back into electrical signals. This process allows for the formation of images based on the echoes received.

slides.title: Now let’s talk about optical imaging, a family of techniques that use visible an
bullets: A key idea is that optical methods can capture biological signatures. For example, with bioluminescence imaging, we use a biological reporter system that produces light inside the body. You can think of it like a firefly: the light is generated by the biology itself, so from the imaging system’s point of view it’s essentially a passive signal that we detect from outside.

Now look at the workflow on this slide. At the bottom, we start with bioluminescence imaging of an animal and collect multiple external views from different angles. Those surface images are useful, but the real goal is harder: we want to recover the 3D distribution of the internal light sources.
That is where optical tomography comes in, and it immediately becomes an inverse problem.
The forward problem is: if we knew the internal light sources and we knew the tissue’s optical properties, we could predict what the camera would measure outside.
The inverse problem is the challenge: given only the external measurements, how do we reconstruct where the sources are inside?

The slide also shows why we often combine optical imaging with computed tomography. CT provides an individualized volume and a shape model of the animal, which tells us the geometry of organs and tissues. From that, we build an optical model that approximates how light scatters and is absorbed through different structures. Once we have that forward model, we can “invert” it to produce a 3D mapping of the bioluminescent sources inside the body.

So the big lesson is the same one you’ve seen across modalities: optical imaging is powerful, but reconstruction is difficult because light scatters strongly in tissue, and that makes the inverse problem sensitive and nontrivial.

Finally, optical imaging is a broad area. Bioluminescence tomography is one example. There is also fluorescence tomography, where we excite a probe with an external light source and detect the emitted signal. And clinically, one of the most important optical techniques is optical coherence tomography (OCT), which is widely used in eye clinics, for example in retinal imaging.

So in summary: optical imaging gives us a unique window into biology, often at the molecular level, and when we push it into 3D, it becomes a classic tomography problem built on forward modeling plus inversion.

slides.title: Now that we’ve talked about how different modalities create images, the next ste
bullets: Take a look at the picture on this slide. You’re not just seeing a raw scan. You’re seeing anatomy with colored regions overlaid, which is a classic output of image analysis. Those colors usually represent structures that have been identified and separated, for example different organs, vessels, or regions of interest. That process is called segmentation.

Segmentation is one of the core tasks in medical image analysis because it turns an image into something you can measure. Once a structure is segmented, you can compute its volume, shape, location, boundaries, and how those change over time. That is what enables things like tracking a tumor, quantifying organ size, or planning a procedure.

A second common task is registration, where we align images from different time points or different modalities, so that “the same location” in the body matches up across scans. That is often how we combine information: anatomy from one scan, function from another, and then interpret them together.

We also use classification and detection to support decisions, for example identifying suspicious patterns or categorizing tissue types. And of course there are enhancement methods, such as denoising or deblurring, which can improve visibility, but we always have to be careful: enhancement should improve interpretability without creating misleading artifacts.

For this course, the main goal is to help you see how analysis fits into the full pipeline. Imaging is not only about forming an image. In practice, a lot of the clinical value comes from what we can extract and quantify from that image: structures, measurements, and interpretable information.

So we will cover the key ideas at a high level, especially the ones that connect directly to imaging systems and reconstruction, and you’ll leave with a clear picture of how acquisition, reconstruction, and analysis fit together in modern medical imaging.

slides.title: To put everything in perspective, let’s take a step back and look at the big pic
bullets: Genotype refers to your genetic makeup—the DNA that gives rise to your traits. Meanwhile, phenotype refers to the observable characteristics of an individual, such as physical traits, biological functions, and disease states. Biomedical imaging plays a crucial role in studying the phenotype. Through imaging, we can visualize and analyze the biological structures and functions that define an individual’s phenotype.

Now, bioinformatics and genetic profiling give us a lot of information about the genotype—things like your genetic predispositions and variations. However, these genetic insights need to be connected to the physical manifestation of the genes—the phenotype. We can use biomedical imaging to observe and understand the effects of genetic factors on the body.

While we’re entering the world of imaging in this course, it’s important to recognize that we’re focusing primarily on phenotype—how imaging allows us to visualize and analyze the body's structures and functions. However, linking genotype (your genetic information) with phenotype (the observable traits and conditions) is an essential goal for personalized medicine. This connection between the two will allow for more accurate diagnosis, more effective treatment, and unprecedented prediction in the future.

slides.title: Looking ahead, our next lecture will focus on building a solid foundation in MAT
bullets: MATLAB is widely used in biomedical engineering and medical imaging for modeling, simulation, and algorithm development, so it will be a practical tool throughout this course. If you do not have MATLAB set up yet, please check your university’s software access options. Many campuses provide MATLAB through a site license, and if you run into installation issues, the university IT resources can usually help.

To prepare, I strongly recommend completing the MATLAB Onramp tutorial. It’s a short, interactive introduction, typically around a couple of hours, and it covers the basics you’ll need: the interface, variables, arrays, plotting, and simple scripts.

So here is your task before the next lecture: finish MATLAB Onramp. By the end of it, you should feel comfortable navigating MATLAB and writing basic commands and scripts. Then, in the next session, we can spend our time applying MATLAB directly to the imaging concepts we care about, instead of getting stuck on the interface.

slides.title: You’ll notice that we’re not treating MATLAB as a side topic in this course. We’
bullets: This slide is just a simple reminder: MATLAB Onramp is a very effective starting point. It walks you through the core skills you will repeatedly need here, things like working with vectors and matrices, indexing, importing data, plotting, and writing basic scripts. Those are not “extra” skills. They are exactly what you use when you start experimenting with signals, convolution, and Fourier transforms.

The bigger point is this: I’m not expecting anyone to learn MATLAB through complicated tricks. The goal is the opposite. If you’re comfortable with the basics, then when we get to homework and projects, you can focus on the imaging concepts instead of struggling with syntax.

One more practical tip: when you’re writing MATLAB code, tools like ChatGPT can be helpful for brainstorming or debugging small pieces of code. But use it wisely, you still need to understand what the code is doing and be able to explain it. Treat it like a support tool, not a replacement for learning.
So, keep building that MATLAB foundation now. The earlier you get comfortable, the smoother everything else in the course will feel.

slides.title: Now let’s zoom out and talk about something that has been reshaping medical imag
bullets: This slide is using a well-known AI milestone as a symbol, the idea that machine learning systems reached a point where they could compete with, and even outperform, top human performance in a complex task. The reason I bring this up is not to talk about that specific event, but to highlight what it represents: a turning point in what data-driven models can do.
In medical imaging, this wave shows up everywhere. AI is helping with:
image reconstruction (making better images from limited or noisy measurements),
image analysis (segmentation, detection, classification),
workflow support (triage and prioritization),
and even robotics and image-guided procedures.

But here is the key message for this course: deep learning is powerful, but it does not replace fundamentals. The best results usually happen when you combine physics, systems thinking, and inverse problems with data-driven models. If you understand the foundations, you will be able to evaluate AI methods critically and use them responsibly, rather than treating them like magic.
So yes, AI is transforming imaging. And that is exactly why learning the core principles still matters.

slides.title: As we move into modern trends, this slide gives a simple but powerful picture of
bullets: What you’re seeing here is the number of papers over time, based on a topic search in Web of Knowledge, over roughly the past 20 years. The blue curve represents medical imaging, and the orange curve represents machine learning.

Both curves are rising, which tells you that medical imaging has been an active and growing research area for a long time. But what’s especially striking is the machine learning curve: it accelerates rapidly and, toward the later years shown on this plot, it begins to catch up with and even surpass medical imaging in publication volume.

The takeaway is not that “one field replaces the other.” The real takeaway is that the intersection is becoming increasingly important. When machine learning, especially deep learning, meets medical imaging, it affects multiple stages of the pipeline:
reconstruction (turning measurements into images more efficiently or with fewer artifacts),
analysis (segmentation, detection, classification),
and interpretation and workflow support (helping clinicians prioritize and quantify).
This is exactly the motivation behind the term “deep imaging”, and that’s why the slide cites the perspective article:
Wang G: “Perspective on Deep Imaging,” IEEE Access, 2016.
The point of that reference is to signal that deep learning is not just an add-on. It has the potential to reshape how we think about both imaging systems and imaging algorithms.

Now, for this course, our priority is still the fundamentals: physics, systems, Fourier ideas, and inverse problems. But if you’re interested in going further, one natural extension is a project where you connect these imaging fundamentals with machine learning ideas in a careful, grounded way. That’s optional, but it can be a great path for students who want a deeper challenge.

slides.title: Building on what we have discussed so far about the intersection of medical imag
bullets: I truly believe the future of medical imaging—and medicine in general—is full of incredible possibilities. As artificial intelligence continues to advance, we’re already seeing systems that can match or even surpass human performance in interpreting medical images. It’s very possible that, in the not-so-distant future, many of the tasks traditionally done by radiologists will be basically handled by AI.

And it goes beyond that. Imagine robotic surgeons, guided by multimodal imaging and machine learning, performing procedures with precision that’s hard for even the best human surgeon to match. Over time, we might see AI and robotics take on more and more roles in healthcare, not to replace professionals, but to enhance what we can achieve—and to make healthcare safer, faster, and more accessible.

This is the direction the field is moving in, and it’s one of the reasons why understanding imaging technologies today is so valuable and timely. You're not just learning how things work now—you’re preparing to shape the future.

slides.title: As we talk about deep learning, AI, and robotics in healthcare, it’s worth remem
bullets: This slide uses Elysium as an example. On the left is the movie poster, and on the right you see that iconic futuristic medical device, basically a “one-stop” system that scans the body, identifies what is wrong, and then fixes it automatically. In the film, it represents an ideal: instant diagnosis, precise treatment, and fully personalized care.

Of course, we’re not there today. That machine is fiction. But the direction it points to is very real. Modern medical imaging is already the foundation of diagnosis. And when you combine imaging with computation, AI, and robotics, you start moving toward systems that are:
more comprehensive (integrating multiple types of information),
more quantitative (measuring instead of guessing),
and more actionable (guiding treatment and interventions).

So the point of this slide is motivation. The dream is not “magic technology.” The dream is building better tools step by step. And that starts with fundamentals: how images are formed, how reconstruction solves inverse problems, how we evaluate quality, and how we extract clinically meaningful information.

As we talk about deep learning, AI, and robotics in healthcare, it’s worth remembering that big scientific ideas often start as dreams, and sometimes science fiction is a useful way to express those dreams.

This slide uses Elysium as an example. On the left is the movie poster, and on the right you see that iconic futuristic medical device, basically a “one-stop” system that scans the body, identifies what is wrong, and then fixes it automatically. In the film, it represents an ideal: instant diagnosis, precise treatment, and fully personalized care.

Of course, we’re not there today. That machine is fiction. But the direction it points to is very real. Modern medical imaging is already the foundation of diagnosis. And when you combine imaging with computation, AI, and robotics, you start moving toward systems that are:

more comprehensive (integrating multiple types of information),

more quantitative (measuring instead of guessing),

and more actionable (guiding treatment and interventions).

So the point of this slide is motivation. The dream is not “magic technology.” The dream is building better tools step by step. And that starts with fundamentals: how images are formed, how reconstruction solves inverse problems, how we evaluate quality, and how we extract clinically meaningful information.

If this vision excites you, that’s great. Because by mastering the foundations in this course, you put yourself in a position to contribute to what comes next, turning parts of that dream into real engineering and real medicine.
slides.script: As we explore the intersection of technology and healthcare, it's essential to recognize that groundbreaking ideas often begin as imaginative concepts, and science fiction can serve as a catalyst for envisioning the future of medicine. The movie Elysium depicts a futuristic scenario where a single device can diagnose and treat diseases, inspiring us to strive for more efficient and effective healthcare solutions. While we're not yet at that level, this vision motivates us to push the boundaries of medical imaging and beyond.
